title: Comparing Growth Rates
subtitle: From O(1) to O(n!) — what each one feels like

sections.heading: Why this lesson matters
sections.body: Two algorithms that both 'work' can behave wildly differently as inputs grow. The difference between O(n) and O(n²) might be invisible at 10 items, but at a million items O(n) finishes while O(n²) is still running when the sun explodes. This lesson burns those scales into your head.

sections.heading: The rough ranking developers memorize
sections.body:
From fastest (best) to slowest (worst):

* **O(1)** — Excellent. Hash map lookup, array index.
* **O(log n)** — Very good. Binary search, balanced tree operations.
* **O(n)** — Fair. A single pass through the data.
* **O(n log n)** — Acceptable. Most efficient sorts.
* **O(n²)** — Bad for large inputs. Nested loops, bubble sort.
* **O(2ⁿ)** — Horrible. Brute-force subsets, naive Fibonacci recursion.
* **O(n!)** — Worst. Brute-force permutations, the traveling salesman.

If you find your code dropping into a worse bucket, that's usually the moment to redesign.

sections.caption: Visual sense of how the curves split

data.text:
operations
      ^         O(n!) ↗
      |       O(2ⁿ) ↗
      |     O(n³)
      |   O(n²)
      |  O(n log n)
      |O(n)
      |O(log n)
      |O(1) ───────────────────────────
      +-------------------------------> n

      O(1) and O(log n) hug the bottom no matter how big n gets.
      O(n²) curves upward sharply.
      O(2ⁿ) and O(n!) shoot off the chart almost immediately.

sections.heading: Rule of thumb for n
sections.body:
A handy mental table for 'will this finish in 1 second?' (1 second ≈ 10⁸ basic operations):

* **n up to 10⁸**: O(n) is fine
* **n up to 10⁶**: O(n log n) is fine
* **n up to 10⁴**: O(n²) is fine
* **n up to ~500**: O(n³) is fine
* **n up to ~25**: O(2ⁿ) is fine
* **n up to ~10**: O(n!) is fine

If your competitive-programming problem says n ≤ 10⁵, you need O(n log n) or better. If n ≤ 20, brute-force exponential is on the table.

questions.question: Which is the FASTEST-growing function?
questions.explanation: Factorial outpaces exponential, which outpaces polynomial. n! > 2ⁿ > n² > n log n.

questions.question: For n = 1,000,000, which complexity gives the fewest operations?
questions.explanation: log₂(10⁶) ≈ 20. Logarithmic absolutely crushes everything else at this scale.

questions.question: What roughly does this print for n = 1000?
questions.explanation: Two nested loops over n each → n² = 10⁶ = 1,000,000 iterations. Classic O(n²).

questions.question: An algorithm processes n=10⁵ items. You want it to finish in 1 second (≈10⁸ ops). The slowest acceptable complexity is O(n ___).
questions.explanation: n log n at n=10⁵ is about 10⁵ · 17 ≈ 1.7 · 10⁶ — comfortably under 10⁸.
